sections.title: Intro and foreigners in Zhongshan
sections.summary: Eileen 和 Kerr 開場，聊到 Kerr 在中山這個小工業城市打籃球時，遇到的南亞和其他外國工作者。
sections.tr: Eileen and Kerr open the show and discuss the South Asian and other foreign workers Kerr meets playing basketball in his small industrial city of Zhongshan.

sections.title: Kerr's close foreign friends
sections.summary: Kerr 講他那一兩個比較要好的外國朋友，包括一個在公司烤肉時聊了好幾個小時、後來還去參加他婚禮的比利時人。
sections.tr: Kerr describes his one or two close foreign friends, including a Belgian he bonded with for hours at a company barbecue and later attended the wedding of.

sections.title: Barriers to foreigners making Chinese friends
sections.summary: 他們解釋為什麼在中國的外國人大多待在封閉的小圈子裡——語言是個障礙，要嘛中文要很好，要嘛得有一個會說英文、又懂文化的中國伴侶。
sections.tr: They explain why foreigners in China stay in closed circles, citing the language barrier and the need for either strong Chinese or an English-speaking, culturally-aware Chinese partner.

sections.title: Kerr's first foreign teachers in high school
sections.summary: Kerr 回憶他最早的外國朋友，也就是高中時換來換去的外籍英文老師，包括一個南斯拉夫人、一個穿曲棍球短褲的加拿大人，還有教攀岩和講聖經故事的 Tony。
sections.tr: Kerr reminisces about his earliest foreign friends, the rotating foreign English teachers in high school, including a Yugoslav, a Canadian in hockey shorts, and Tony who taught climbing and Bible stories.

sections.title: Privacy and nosy small-talk questions
sections.summary: 他們把跟外國人能聊得很深，對比中國人愛問的那些很私人的問題——結婚了沒、薪水多少、房子多大、錢的事——Kerr 覺得這些既愛打聽又沒什麼意義。
sections.tr: They contrast deep talk with foreigners against the typical intrusive Chinese small-talk about marriage, salary, housing size, and personal finances that Kerr finds prying and pointless.

sections.title: Why Chinese people ask about money
sections.summary: 他們聊到計程車司機會大方說自己收入很低，有些人卻裝有錢（提到恆大的許家印），並把這種對錢的執著，連到中國雖然 GDP 很大、平均收入卻偏低。
sections.tr: They discuss how taxi drivers openly share their low incomes while others fake wealth (citing Evergrande's Xu Jiayin), and link the obsession with money to China's low average income despite a huge GDP.

sections.title: No time for hobbies vs. foreigners' leisure
sections.summary: 他們認為經濟壓力讓一般中國人沒時間也沒精力培養興趣，而比較有錢的外國人、受過教育的專業人士和學生，反而能發展嗜好，聊起來也更有深度。
sections.tr: They argue that financial pressure leaves ordinary Chinese no time or energy for hobbies, whereas wealthier foreigners, educated professionals, and students can cultivate interests that make for deeper conversation.

sections.title: What foreigners talk about instead
sections.summary: 他們說外國人很少追問收入，反而會問你最近過得如何、看了什麼電影、有什麼有趣的事，而這些話題 Kerr 的中國熟人大多覺得很無聊。
sections.tr: They note foreigners rarely probe income and instead ask how you're doing, what films you've seen, or interesting events, topics most of Kerr's Chinese acquaintances find uninteresting.

sections.title: Lying to deflect prying questions
sections.summary: Kerr 承認他對愛打聽的熟人從不老實，講他怎麼閃球友和店老闆問女朋友的事，還有同事一直想幫他介紹對象。
sections.tr: Kerr admits he is never honest with prying acquaintances, recounting dodging questions about his girlfriend from basketball buddies and a shop owner, and colleagues pushing to set him up with a girlfriend.

sections.title: Weak boundaries and blunt appearance comments
sections.summary: 他們聊到中國人界線很模糊，會隨口評論別人的頭髮、衣服、膚色、胖瘦，甚至對黑人問很沒禮貌的問題，相對之下國際上和東南亞的態度比較包容。
sections.tr: They discuss how Chinese people have weak boundaries, freely commenting on hair, clothes, skin color, weight, and even crude questions to Black people, versus more tolerant international and Southeast Asian attitudes.

sections.title: Surface-level friendship and refusing to debate
sections.summary: 他們解釋中國人常先入為主，覺得外國人差太多、聊不來，也不願意辯論——像是有個朋友因為 Kerr 喜歡鋼彈裡的反派而生氣，另一個則死不相信某些國際上發生的事。
sections.tr: They explain how Chinese people preset foreigners as too different to connect with and shut down debate, illustrated by a friend angered over Kerr liking the Gundam villains and another refusing to believe world events.

sections.title: Debate, open-mindedness, and stereotypes
sections.summary: 他們稱讚外國人能就事論事地辯論、不帶恨意，還能從不同意見中成長；接著談到中國人對外國人的刻板印象——以為他們思想開放、性觀念也開放，有些老外乾脆就在網路上演這個樣子。
sections.tr: They praise how foreigners debate without hatred and grow from opposing views, then examine Chinese stereotypes that foreigners are open-minded and sexually liberal, which some expats end up performing online.

sections.title: Social activities and lack of outdoor culture
sections.summary: 他們比較雙方的社交活動，說中國男生大多就是吃飯、喝酒、唱 KTV，不太願意去爬山或散步，並把戶外嗜好少的原因歸在時間、錢和場地都有限。
sections.tr: They compare social activities, noting Chinese men mostly do dinners, drinking, and KTV while rejecting hiking or walks, and blame the scarcity of outdoor hobbies on limited time, money, and venues.

sections.title: English-practice friendships and favor-trading
sections.summary: 他們回應外國人的抱怨——說中國人交朋友只是為了練英文、把人當工具；也聊到中國人還人情的方式，是靠累積的人情和請唱 KTV，而不是直接給錢。
sections.tr: They address foreigners' complaint that Chinese befriend them just to practice English, treating people as tools, and discuss how Chinese repay favors through saved-up obligations and KTV rather than cash.

sections.title: Money, lending, and a betrayed friendship
sections.summary: 他們把西方算錢算得很清楚，對比中國人不喜歡打借據、錢的事含糊不清；Kerr 還講到自己被一個借了好幾千塊的朋友一再佔便宜，最後乾脆絕交。
sections.tr: They contrast clear Western money dealings with the blurry Chinese aversion to IOUs, and Kerr recounts being repeatedly exploited by a friend he lent thousands to, whom he finally cut off.

sections.title: Exploiting friendship for free work
sections.summary: 他們感嘆把交情和錢摻在一起就容易被背叛，也批評有些中國老闆會利用關係，叫人免費做設計或商標，結果做得很差、反而更花錢。
sections.tr: They lament that mixing friendship with money invites betrayal, and criticize Chinese bosses who exploit connections for free design or logo work that ends up done badly and costing more.

sections.title: Age hierarchy as a barrier to friendship
sections.summary: 他們聊到中國人很在意「什麼年紀該有什麼樣子」，還有叔叔、大哥這類稱呼，這種輩分觀念會擋住不同年齡之間的平等友誼。
sections.tr: They discuss how the Chinese fixation on age-appropriate behavior and honorific titles like uncle or big brother creates hierarchy that blocks equal friendships across age gaps.

sections.title: Preferred nationalities and closed-minded encounters
sections.summary: Kerr 說他最喜歡的朋友是東南亞人，因為他們比較國際化、又會自嘲；他把沒耐性或有偏見的美國人，對比那些少數很有耐心的；最後談到那些對中國大陸人關上門、不願往來的外國人。
sections.tr: Kerr names Southeast Asians as his favorite friends for their international, self-deprecating humor, and contrasts impatient or prejudiced Americans with the rare patient ones, closing on foreigners who shut the door on mainland Chinese.
